---
title: "Lab 03 - Tidy Data and Data Transformation - INSTRUCTIONS"
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set( echo = TRUE )
```

# Overview

In this lab, students will continue developing data wrangling skills by learning how to transform messy datasets into tidy, analysis-ready formats. The lab is divided into two parts. First, students will work through guided examples using an instructor-provided dataset to practice reshaping data and cleaning string and date variables. Second, students will apply the same techniques to the dataset they selected earlier in the course.

The purpose of this lab is to help students understand how data structure affects analysis and how standardized workflows improve reproducibility and transparency.

Learning Objectives

By completing this lab, students will be able to:

Identify characteristics of tidy and untidy data
Reshape datasets using pivot_longer() and pivot_wider()
Clean and standardize string variables using stringr
Transform and format date variables using lubridate
Apply reproducible data transformation workflows to real-world data
Document data transformation decisions in RMarkdown
Part 1: Guided Data Transformation Practice
Instructor-Provided Dataset

For the first portion of the lab, use the dataset provided by the instructor. Follow the guided examples to practice restructuring and cleaning data using Tidyverse tools.

Tasks
1. Inspect the Dataset Structure

Load the dataset and examine its structure using functions such as:

glimpse()
summary()
head()

Identify examples of untidy or inconsistent data formatting.

2. Reshape the Dataset

Use the following functions to restructure the dataset:

pivot_longer()
pivot_wider()

After each transformation, briefly explain:

What changed in the structure of the dataset
Why the transformation may be useful for analysis
3. Clean String Variables

Use functions from stringr to clean or standardize text variables.

Examples may include:

Converting text to upper or lower case
Removing extra spaces or punctuation
Extracting portions of strings
Standardizing category labels
4. Clean and Transform Date Variables

Use lubridate functions to:

Convert dates into standardized formats
Extract components such as year, month, or weekday
Create new date-related variables
5. Create a Tidy Dataset

Produce a cleaned and transformed version of the instructor-provided dataset that is ready for analysis.

Part 2: Apply the Workflow to Your Dataset
Using Your Dataset

Now apply the same workflow to the dataset you selected earlier in the course.

Tasks
1. Identify Untidy or Inconsistent Features

Describe at least two issues in your dataset related to:

Data structure
String formatting
Date formatting
Variable consistency
2. Apply Data Transformation Techniques

Use at least three of the following functions or approaches:

pivot_longer()
pivot_wider()
String cleaning with stringr
Date cleaning with lubridate

Your transformations should meaningfully improve the usability of the dataset.

3. Create a Cleaned Version of the Dataset

Produce a transformed version of your dataset that is more consistent and analysis-ready.

4. Record a Short Walkthrough Video

Create a short video presentation (approximately 3–5 minutes) explaining the transformations you performed on your dataset.

Your video should include:

A brief introduction to your dataset
Examples of untidy or inconsistent data
A demonstration of at least two transformations you performed
An explanation of why those transformations were necessary
A brief reflection on challenges encountered during the cleaning process

Your screen recording should clearly show your RStudio workflow and include narration describing your decisions.

RMarkdown Report

Document your work in an RMarkdown report that includes:

Section headers for Part 1 and Part 2
Code chunks showing all transformations
Brief explanations of each step
Before-and-after examples of cleaned variables or reshaped data
A short reflection describing what you learned about data transformation and tidy data

The report should include both code and output.

Deliverables

Submit the following:

Your completed RMarkdown (.Rmd) file
A rendered version of the report (.html, .pdf, or .docx)
Your cleaned or transformed dataset file
A link or upload of your walkthrough video
Looking Ahead

The cleaned and transformed datasets produced in this lab will be used in upcoming weeks for:

Data visualization with ggplot2
Exploratory data analysis
Interactive dashboards with Shiny
Reproducible analytical workflows using the Tidyverse






NEED IN STRUCTIONS AGAIN ON HOW TO MAKE THE VIDEO!













# How to Submit

Download the [template for this lab](../labs/templates/lab-03-LastName.zip?raw=true) prior to beginning the lab. 

<br>

### Knitting to HTML

When you have completed your assignment, click the "Knit" button to render your `.RMD` file into a `.HTML` report.

<br>

### Special Instructions

Upload both your `.RMD` and `.HTML` files to the appropriate link for this assignment on the Canvas page for this course.

<br>

### Before You Submit

Remember to ensure the following before submitting your assignment.

  * Name your files using this format: **Lab-##-LastName.rmd** and **Lab-##-LastName.html**
  * Show both the solution for your code and write out your answers in the body text 

See [Google's R Style Guide](https://google.github.io/styleguide/Rguide.xml) for examples of common conventions.

<br>
<br>

### Common Knitting Issues

`.RMD` files are knit into `.HTML` and other formats procedural, or line-by-line.

* An error in code when knitting will halt the process; error messages will tell you the specific line with the error
* Certain functions, like `install.packages()` or `setwd()`, are bound to cause errors in knitting 
* Altering a dataset or variable in one chunk will affect its use in all later chunks
* If an object is "not found", make sure it was created or loaded with `library()` in a previous chunk

**If All Else Fails:** If you cannot determine and fix the errors in a code chunk that's preventing you from knitting your document, add `eval = FALSE` inside the brackets of `{r}` at the beginning of a chunk to ensure that R does not attempt to evaluate it, that is: `{r eval = FALSE}`. This will prevent an erroneous chunk of code from halting the knitting process.
